fields.brief_block: The Court drew a careful distinction between Feiner v. New York, 340 U.S. 315 (1951) — where a speaker had genuinely incited near-riot conditions, the crowd was surging, and police authority was being actively challenged — and an ordinary peaceful protest attended by verbal disagreements.
fields.opinion_block: It is one thing to say that the police cannot be used as an instrument for the suppression of unpopular views, and another to say that, when as here the speaker passes the bounds of argument or persuasion and undertakes incitement to riot, they are powerless to prevent a breach of the peace.
fields.finding_analysis: Feiner v. New York is a free-speech/breach-of-peace case in which the Supreme Court affirmed a disorderly-conduct conviction, and its facts match the brief's characterization. The opinion recounts that Feiner urged his listeners "to rise up in arms and fight for equal rights," that the crowd was "restless" with "pushing, shoving and milling around" and "pressing closer around petitioner and the officer," that at least one onlooker "threatened violence if the police did not act," and that petitioner engaged in "deliberate defiance of the police officers" by refusing repeated orders. The Court expressly drew the very distinction the brief describes, contrasting "the ordinary murmurings and objections of a hostile audience," which "cannot be allowed to silence a speaker," with the situation "as here" where "the speaker passes the bounds of argument or persuasion and undertakes incitement to riot." The brief's use of Feiner as a near-riot, active-defiance contrast to an ordinary peaceful protest is a fair and accurate reading of the holding.
